questions.question: What is the fundamental speed difference between stack and heap allocation?
options: Stack: one CPU instruction (move RSP); heap: searching free space, ~50-200+ cycles
questions.correctAnswer: Stack: one CPU instruction (move RSP); heap: searching free space, ~50-200+ cycles
questions.explanation: Note: 'Stack allocation is automatic, one CPU instruction. Heap involves searching for free space.'

questions.question: What determines a stack variable's lifetime?
options: Tied to function scope — destroyed when function returns
questions.correctAnswer: Tied to function scope — destroyed when function returns
questions.explanation: Note: 'Stack lifetime: tied to function scope. Heap: as long as you want — until delete.'

questions.question: What is the typical maximum stack size?
questions.correctAnswer: 1-8 MB (platform-dependent)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Stack max ~1-8 MB. Heap: gigabytes. Large data must go on heap.'

questions.question: What register tracks the top of the stack on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: RSP
questions.explanation: Note: 'The CPU tracks the top with a register called the stack pointer (RSP on x86-64).'

questions.question: Why is the stack cache-friendly?
options: LIFO — the top is always in L1/L2 cache, recently used memory is hot
questions.correctAnswer: LIFO — the top is always in L1/L2 cache, recently used memory is hot
questions.explanation: The stack's LIFO nature means the most recently pushed data stays cache-hot.

questions.question: What fragmentation pattern does the stack have?
questions.correctAnswer: None — LIFO allocation/deallocation prevents fragmentation
questions.explanation: Note table: Stack fragmentation = None (LIFO). Heap can fragment over time.

questions.question: What must be known at compile time for a stack variable?
questions.correctAnswer: Its size (for arrays/VLAs)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Variable size must be known at compile time on the stack. Heap can be determined at runtime.'

questions.question: Why is sizeof(struct{char a; int b; char c;}) typically 12, not 6?
options: Padding — CPU requires aligned access; compiler inserts invisible bytes
questions.correctAnswer: Padding — CPU requires aligned access; compiler inserts invisible bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'Six bytes of data, twelve bytes of object. Padding. A 4-byte int must live at an address divisible by 4.'

questions.question: What hardware constraint drives alignment requirements?
options: CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks — misaligned data requires two reads
questions.correctAnswer: CPUs read memory in aligned word chunks — misaligned data requires two reads
questions.explanation: Note: 'CPUs don't read byte-by-byte. Misaligned int crossing a word boundary needs two memory accesses.'

questions.question: How can you reduce padding in a struct?
options: Order members from largest to smallest alignment
questions.correctAnswer: Order members from largest to smallest alignment
questions.explanation: Placing larger-alignment members first reduces padding gaps.

questions.question: What is the alignment of a double on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 8 bytes
questions.explanation: Note: 'A type of size N must live at an address divisible by N (up to pointer size).' double is 8 bytes.

questions.question: What does AoS vs SoA stand for and which is faster for SIMD?
options: Array of Structures vs Structure of Arrays — SoA is often faster for SIMD
questions.correctAnswer: Array of Structures vs Structure of Arrays — SoA is often faster for SIMD
questions.explanation: Structure of Arrays keeps same-type fields contiguous for better cache and SIMD utilization.

questions.question: What does `alignof(T)` return?
options: The required memory alignment of type T — the address must be divisible by this value
The number of T objects in a cache line
The offset of T within a struct
questions.correctAnswer: The required memory alignment of type T — the address must be divisible by this value
questions.explanation: Note: alignof returns the alignment requirement. alignof(int) = 4, alignof(double) = 8.

questions.question: What happens to a struct's size when you use `#pragma pack(1)`?
options: All padding is removed — members are packed tightly with no gaps
questions.correctAnswer: All padding is removed — members are packed tightly with no gaps
questions.explanation: #pragma pack(1) forces byte-alignment, removing padding. Tradeoff: potentially slower access due to misalignment.

questions.question: Why don't raw pointers auto-clean heap memory?
options: The pointer variable (8 bytes) is destroyed, but the heap object it pointed to is NOT
questions.correctAnswer: The pointer variable (8 bytes) is destroyed, but the heap object it pointed to is NOT
questions.explanation: Note: 'When pointer goes out of scope, the pointer VARIABLE is destroyed. Heap memory is NOT.'

questions.question: What RAII principle do smart pointers implement?
options: Tie resource lifetime to object lifetime — destructor frees automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Tie resource lifetime to object lifetime — destructor frees automatically
questions.explanation: Smart pointer destructor deletes the managed object when the smart pointer goes out of scope.

questions.question: What is unique about std::unique_ptr ownership?
options: Only one unique_ptr owns the object — can't be copied, only moved
questions.correctAnswer: Only one unique_ptr owns the object — can't be copied, only moved
questions.explanation: unique_ptr enforces exclusive ownership. Copy deleted; only move allowed. Move transfers ownership.

questions.question: When use std::shared_ptr over std::unique_ptr?
options: When multiple owners need shared access — last one to die cleans up
questions.correctAnswer: When multiple owners need shared access — last one to die cleans up
questions.explanation: shared_ptr uses reference counting. Object deleted when last shared_ptr is destroyed.

questions.question: What is std::weak_ptr used for?
options: Breaking reference cycles in shared_ptr graphs — doesn't affect refcount
questions.correctAnswer: Breaking reference cycles in shared_ptr graphs — doesn't affect refcount
questions.explanation: weak_ptr observes without owning. Essential for breaking circular references that cause memory leaks.

questions.question: What does std::make_unique<T>() do?
options: Creates a new T without allocation
Allocates and constructs a T, returns unique_ptr<T> — exception-safe, single allocation
questions.correctAnswer: Allocates and constructs a T, returns unique_ptr<T> — exception-safe, single allocation
questions.explanation: make_unique is the recommended way to create unique_ptrs. Avoids raw new and is exception-safe.

questions.question: What is the overhead of std::shared_ptr compared to a raw pointer?
options: Stores two pointers: one to object, one to control block (refcount + deleter) — 16 bytes vs 8
questions.correctAnswer: Stores two pointers: one to object, one to control block (refcount + deleter) — 16 bytes vs 8
questions.explanation: shared_ptr is 16 bytes (ptr + control block ptr). unique_ptr is 8 bytes (same as raw pointer).

questions.question: Can you use std::unique_ptr with C-style arrays?
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — std::unique_ptr<int[]> correctly calls delete[]
questions.explanation: unique_ptr<T[]> specialization calls delete[] instead of delete. Shared_ptr didn't support arrays until C++17.

questions.question: What does RAII stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization
questions.explanation: Note: 'RAII means tie the lifetime of a resource to the lifetime of an object. Constructor acquires, destructor releases.'

questions.question: What are the five special member functions in the Rule of Five?
questions.correctAnswer: Destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, move assignment
acceptableAnswers: destructor copy constructor copy assignment move constructor move assignment
~T(), T(const T&), T& operator=(const T&), T(T&&), T& operator=(T&&)
questions.explanation: Rule of Five: if you define any of the five, define all five for consistent resource management.

questions.question: Why does the Rule of Five exist?
options: If you need a custom destructor (resource ownership), compiler-generated copy/move might shallow-copy your resource
questions.correctAnswer: If you need a custom destructor (resource ownership), compiler-generated copy/move might shallow-copy your resource
questions.explanation: Custom destructor signals resource ownership. Default copy/move copies the pointer, not the resource.

questions.question: What is the Rule of Zero?
options: Don't declare special members — use RAII types (unique_ptr, vector, string) so compiler-generated ones are correct
questions.correctAnswer: Don't declare special members — use RAII types (unique_ptr, vector, string) so compiler-generated ones are correct
questions.explanation: Rule of Zero: all RAII members = correct compiler-generated destructor/copy/move.

questions.question: In what three situations are destructors guaranteed to run?
questions.correctAnswer: Normal scope exit, exception unwinding, and early return/break/continue
questions.explanation: Note: destructors run on normal exit, exception unwinding (stack unwinding), and any control flow leaving scope.

questions.question: What kinds of resources can RAII manage?
questions.correctAnswer: Memory, files, locks, sockets, database connections, GPU buffers — anything with acquire/release
questions.explanation: Note lists: 'Memory (new/delete), files (open/close), locks (lock/unlock), sockets, database connections, GPU buffers.'

questions.question: What happens to RAII-managed resources if an exception is thrown?
options: Destructors still run during stack unwinding — resources are released
questions.correctAnswer: Destructors still run during stack unwinding — resources are released
questions.explanation: Note: 'Even if an exception is thrown, destructors run during stack unwinding. Resources are always released.'

questions.question: What is the key advantage of std::array over a C-style array?
options: Same memory layout but adds .size(), .at() bounds checking, never decays to pointer
questions.correctAnswer: Same memory layout but adds .size(), .at() bounds checking, never decays to pointer
questions.explanation: Zero-overhead: same contiguous memory as C array, but with container interface.

questions.question: What happens to a C array's size when passed to a function?
options: Decays to pointer — sizeof becomes 8 (pointer size), array size lost
questions.correctAnswer: Decays to pointer — sizeof becomes 8 (pointer size), array size lost
questions.explanation: C arrays decay to pointers. sizeof(arr) in a function returns pointer size, not array size.

questions.question: How do you get the size of a std::array at compile time?
questions.correctAnswer: All three work
questions.explanation: std::array::size() is constexpr. sizeof gives bytes. tuple_size gives element count at compile time.

questions.question: Can std::array be used with range-for loops?
options: Yes — it has begin() and end() iterators
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — it has begin() and end() iterators
questions.explanation: std::array is a full container with begin()/end(), works with range-for and all STL algorithms.

questions.question: Does `std::array<int, 5> arr;` initialize its elements?
options: No — elements are uninitialized (garbage) unless value-initialized with {}
questions.correctAnswer: No — elements are uninitialized (garbage) unless value-initialized with {}
questions.explanation: Like C arrays, std::array without initializer leaves elements uninitialized. Use {} for zero-initialization.

questions.question: What three pointers does std::vector store internally?
questions.correctAnswer: begin, end (size), end of storage (capacity)
questions.explanation: sizeof(vector) = 24 bytes on 64-bit. begin→end = size, end→capacity_end = spare capacity.

questions.question: What is the growth factor of std::vector?
questions.correctAnswer: 1.5x (MSVC) or 2x (GCC/Clang)
questions.explanation: Must be exponential for amortized O(1) push_back. MSVC uses 1.5x for better memory reuse.

questions.question: What's the difference between size() and capacity()?
options: size() = stored elements; capacity() = allocated slots before reallocation
questions.correctAnswer: size() = stored elements; capacity() = allocated slots before reallocation
questions.explanation: capacity ≥ size. When size exceeds capacity, reallocation occurs.

questions.question: What does reserve(n) do?
options: Pre-allocates capacity to at least n — prevents reallocations until size exceeds n
questions.correctAnswer: Pre-allocates capacity to at least n — prevents reallocations until size exceeds n
questions.explanation: reserve() is the key to vector performance: allocate once, push many times without reallocation.

questions.question: What does emplace_back do differently from push_back?
options: Constructs the element in-place at the end — avoids a temporary + move/copy
questions.correctAnswer: Constructs the element in-place at the end — avoids a temporary + move/copy
questions.explanation: emplace_back forwards constructor arguments directly. push_back creates a temporary first.

questions.question: What data structure does std::map use?
questions.correctAnswer: Red-black tree (self-balancing BST)
questions.explanation: std::map is ordered. O(log n) operations. Elements sorted by key.

questions.question: What data structure does std::unordered_map use?
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table with chaining
questions.explanation: std::unordered_map uses hashing. O(1) average, O(n) worst case.

questions.question: When should you use map vs unordered_map?
options: map for ordered iteration/sorted keys; unordered_map for fastest lookup without ordering
questions.correctAnswer: map for ordered iteration/sorted keys; unordered_map for fastest lookup without ordering
questions.explanation: map: O(log n), sorted. unordered_map: O(1) average, unsorted, higher memory overhead.

questions.question: What operator allows map[key] = value syntax?
questions.correctAnswer: operator[]
questions.explanation: map[key] returns reference to value. Inserts default-constructed value if key doesn't exist.

questions.question: What complexity does std::map::find() have?
questions.correctAnswer: O(log n)
questions.explanation: Red-black tree: all operations (find, insert, erase) are O(log n).

questions.question: What are the five iterator categories?
questions.correctAnswer: Input, Output, Forward, Bidirectional, Random Access
acceptableAnswers: input output forward bidirectional random access
questions.explanation: Each adds capabilities: input (read-once), output (write-once), forward (multi-pass), bidirectional (--), random access ([] and +n).

questions.question: What does std::sort require from iterators?
questions.correctAnswer: Random access
questions.explanation: std::sort needs O(1) element access for partitioning — only random access iterators provide this.

questions.question: What does std::find return if element not found?
questions.correctAnswer: The end iterator
questions.explanation: All search algorithms return the end iterator to indicate not found. Always compare against container.end().

questions.question: What is the time complexity of std::sort?
questions.correctAnswer: O(n log n)
questions.explanation: std::sort uses introsort (quicksort + heapsort fallback). O(n log n) average and worst case.

questions.question: What does std::accumulate do?
options: Sums elements (or applies any binary operation cumulatively)
questions.correctAnswer: Sums elements (or applies any binary operation cumulatively)
questions.explanation: std::accumulate is a reduction: applies a binary operation cumulatively. Default is addition.
